body: These regression tests cover the pre-instantiated-estimator path, but they only use LinearRegressionEstimator for both stages. Since issue #1335 was triggered specifically with a pre-instantiated GeneralizedLinearModelEstimator (and relies on estimator-specific state like glm_family), adding at least one test that uses GeneralizedLinearModelEstimator as a pre-instantiated stage model would better guard against regressions in the reported scenario.

Same issue as first-stage cloning: second_stage_model.get_new_estimator_object(modified_target_estimand) is called without forwarding TwoStageRegressionEstimator’s test_significance / evaluate_effect_strength / confidence_intervals, so pre-instantiated models can behave differently than class-based models. Consider passing these flags explicitly when cloning.

When cloning a pre-instantiated stage estimator via get_new_estimator_object(modified_target_estimand), the stage model’s configuration flags (test_significance / evaluate_effect_strength / confidence_intervals) are not aligned with the TwoStageRegressionEstimator instance. get_new_estimator_object defaults test_significance/evaluate_effect_strength to False, which can silently change behavior compared to the code path where a class is passed (where these flags are explicitly forwarded). Pass the current estimator’s flags into get_new_estimator_object so both code paths behave consistently.

Added comprehensive warning tests in commit `89a2361`. The new test file `tests/causal_refuters/test_nonparametric_sensitivity_warnings.py` uses `caplog` to verify that WARNING log records are emitted for:
- Low outcome R² (`r2y_tw < 0.1`)
- Low treatment R² (`r2t_w < 0.1`)
- Invalid S² (non-positive)
- Non-finite outcome variance (zero variance case)

Each test creates specific data conditions to trigger the warnings and asserts the expected log messages are present.

Implemented the requested robustness updates in `check_sensitivity()`: `r2y_tw` now guards zero/non-finite variance before division, and both `r2y_tw`/`r2t_w` warnings now trigger for non-finite values as well as low values. Addressed in commit `fbd7684` (with related refinements in `ad02569`, `c37b90c`, and `9b555de`).

This change introduces new supported behavior (partial/empty `independence_test` dicts), but current tests only cover the case where both keys are provided. Please add tests that pass (a) only `test_for_discrete`, (b) only `test_for_continuous`, and (c) `{}` to ensure the new fallback behavior is exercised.

The defaults ("partial_correlation" / "conditional_mutual_information") are duplicated here and must stay in sync with `GraphRefuter.__init__` defaults. To avoid future drift, consider only passing `method_name_continuous`/`method_name_discrete` when the corresponding key is present in `independence_test`, and otherwise rely on `GraphRefuter`'s own defaults.

The new comment suggests this supports “non-binary treatments (e.g., treatment_value=2)”. However, PropensityScoreEstimator.fit() hard-rejects any treatment values outside {0,1} (see propensity_score_estimator.py:133-137). Consider rewording this to “non-default binary encodings / reversed coding” (or similar) to avoid implying unsupported behavior.
@copilot apply changes based on [this feedback](https://github.com/py-why/dowhy/pull/1511#discussion_r3213769355)
@copilot apply changes based on [this feedback](https://github.com/py-why/dowhy/pull/1511#discussion_r3213769349)

`assert result is not None` is a very weak end-to-end assertion and may not catch behavioral regressions (e.g., returning an unexpected object or a result missing expected fields). Consider asserting on a stable, user-relevant invariant of the returned refutation object (e.g., expected type or presence of key attributes/fields) while keeping the test resilient to minor formatting changes.

These regression tests currently only verify that no exception is raised. To make them more robust against future regressions (e.g., returning `None`, `nan`, or a non-numeric type), consider asserting basic properties of the return value (such as being a finite number).

`x`/`y` were fixed for string column names, but `z` still has the same failure mode if callers pass a single string (e.g., `z='Foo'`), because `list(z)` will split it into characters and likely trigger a `KeyError`. Consider accepting both `str` and `Sequence[str]`, e.g., normalize with `t = [z] if isinstance(z, str) else list(z)`, or raise a clear `TypeError` if `z` is not a list-like of column names.

The docstring for `identify_effect_id` uses outdated parameter names (`treatment_names`/`outcome_names`) and does not mention that `graph` can now be a `CausalGraph` wrapper. Since this function is part of the public API, please align the docstring/type hints with the actual arguments (`action_nodes`/`outcome_nodes`) and the newly supported graph input type.

`identify_effect_auto` now accepts a `CausalGraph` wrapper at runtime, but the function signature (and the `AutoIdentifier.identify_effect` signature) still type `graph` as `nx.DiGraph`. Please update the type hints and/or docstring to reflect that `graph` may be a `CausalGraph` (or generally an object exposing an underlying `nx.DiGraph`), so users and static type checkers get accurate guidance.

This PR adds a new categorical-treatment fallback path for empty covariates, but the added tests only cover binary and continuous treatments. Add a regression test that exercises `method=\"weighting\"` with an empty adjustment set and a categorical treatment (`\"o\"/\"u\"/\"d\"`) to prevent regressions in this new branch.

`get_type_string(...)` returns a string, so `\"\".join(dep_type)` is redundant and a bit misleading (it reads like `dep_type` might be a list). Use `var_type=dep_type` directly to keep the intent clear.

The PR description notes pre-existing test failures because newer `sklearn` versions removed the `multi_class=\"ovr\"` kwarg for `LogisticRegression`. Since this code is in the touched hunk, consider updating the call to be compatible across `sklearn` versions (e.g., avoid passing `multi_class`, or gate it based on supported parameters) to prevent runtime errors in environments with newer `sklearn`.

`np` is used in the new no-covariates branch but isn’t imported in the shown module imports, which will raise `NameError` at runtime. Add a `numpy` import (e.g., `import numpy as np`) at module scope.

When `nodes` is provided, there is no validation that each requested node exists in `causal_model.graph.nodes`. For unknown nodes, `is_root_node(...)` / `get_ordered_predecessors(...)` will raise a `networkx` exception, or pandas will raise `KeyError` when indexing `anomaly_data`, which is hard to interpret. Consider validating `nodes` up-front (e.g., check `set(nodes).issubset(causal_model.graph.nodes)` and raise `ValueError` listing missing nodes) so callers get a clear, consistent error.

`nodes` is typed as `Collection[Any]`, which includes `str`. If a caller passes `nodes="X1"` (a common mistake), the function will iterate over characters ('X', '1') and then fail with confusing downstream errors. Consider either (a) accepting `Union[Any, Collection[Any]]` and normalizing scalars to a 1-element list, or (b) explicitly rejecting `str`/`bytes` with a clear `TypeError`/`ValueError`.

The fix updates both `first_stage_model` and `second_stage_model` when pre-instantiated estimators are passed, but the added regression tests only cover the pre-instantiated `second_stage_model` path. Add a complementary test that passes a pre-instantiated `first_stage_model` (and asserts it no longer crashes + its `_target_estimand.identifier_method` is updated to `\"backdoor\"`) to ensure both branches remain covered.

The fix updates both `first_stage_model` and `second_stage_model` when pre-instantiated estimators are passed, but the added regression tests only cover the pre-instantiated `second_stage_model` path. Add a complementary test that passes a pre-instantiated `first_stage_model` (and asserts it no longer crashes + its `_target_estimand.identifier_method` is updated to `\"backdoor\"`) to ensure both branches remain covered.

The fix updates both `first_stage_model` and `second_stage_model` when pre-instantiated estimators are passed, but the added regression tests only cover the pre-instantiated `second_stage_model` path. Add a complementary test that passes a pre-instantiated `first_stage_model` (and asserts it no longer crashes + its `_target_estimand.identifier_method` is updated to `\"backdoor\"`) to ensure both branches remain covered.

Mutating `_target_estimand` on a user-supplied estimator instance has externally visible side effects (the caller’s estimator object is changed after passing it in). Consider avoiding mutation by cloning the estimator before updating the estimand (e.g., `copy.deepcopy` when feasible), or explicitly documenting (in the `TwoStageRegressionEstimator` docstring / parameter docs) that pre-instantiated stage estimators will be modified in-place.

Mutating `_target_estimand` on a user-supplied estimator instance has externally visible side effects (the caller’s estimator object is changed after passing it in). Consider avoiding mutation by cloning the estimator before updating the estimand (e.g., `copy.deepcopy` when feasible), or explicitly documenting (in the `TwoStageRegressionEstimator` docstring / parameter docs) that pre-instantiated stage estimators will be modified in-place.

This introduces an aliasing bug when the same pre-instantiated estimator instance is passed for both `first_stage_model` and `second_stage_model`: the second assignment overwrites `_target_estimand` and both stage references end up sharing the same estimator with the second-stage estimand. Fix by detecting `first_stage_model is second_stage_model` (when both are instances) and either (a) creating a separate copy for one stage (e.g., via `copy.deepcopy`) or (b) raising a clear `ValueError` requiring distinct instances.

This introduces an aliasing bug when the same pre-instantiated estimator instance is passed for both `first_stage_model` and `second_stage_model`: the second assignment overwrites `_target_estimand` and both stage references end up sharing the same estimator with the second-stage estimand. Fix by detecting `first_stage_model is second_stage_model` (when both are instances) and either (a) creating a separate copy for one stage (e.g., via `copy.deepcopy`) or (b) raising a clear `ValueError` requiring distinct instances.

New warning behavior in `check_sensitivity()` is currently untested. Since the repo already uses `caplog` to assert warnings, consider adding targeted tests that trigger low `r2y_tw`/`r2t_w` (and invalid `S2`) and assert the expected WARNING log records are emitted.

The low-R² warnings won’t trigger if the computed value is non-finite (e.g., `np.var(Y)==0` or `np.var(T)==0` leading to `inf`/`nan` from the variance ratio). To make these quality checks robust (and avoid divide-by-zero runtime warnings), guard the variance denominator and/or use a condition like `if (not np.isfinite(self.r2y_tw)) or (self.r2y_tw < 0.1):` (same for `r2t_w`).

`self.S = np.sqrt(self.S2)` is executed before checking whether `S2` is positive/finite. If `S2 <= 0` this will emit a NumPy runtime warning and set `self.S` to NaN, which then propagates into later calculations that divide by `self.S`. Consider validating `self.S2` (e.g., `<= 0` or non-finite) before taking the square root, and setting `self.S` explicitly (or early-returning) when invalid.

The new parallel-fit test only asserts the learned parameters for node `X1` (single-parent regression). Since `fit(..., n_jobs>1)` also fits `X2` with two parents, consider asserting `X2`'s learned coefficients/intercept as well to cover the multi-parent fitting path in the threaded execution.

`n_jobs` defaults to `None`, but the implementation maps `None` to `config.default_n_jobs` (currently `-1`), which means `fit()` will run in parallel by default. This changes the previous default (serial fitting) and also contradicts the PR description claiming the default preserves existing serial behavior. To preserve backward compatibility, consider making the default effectively serial (e.g., default to `1`, or treat `n_jobs is None` as `1` and require an explicit opt-in for parallelism) and update the docstring accordingly.

This only updates the supplied instance used for NIE. For `NONPARAMETRIC_NDE`, `__init__` still creates `_second_stage_model_nde` later with `type(self._second_stage_model)(...)`, which drops any constructor-specific state from a pre-instantiated estimator (for example `GeneralizedLinearModelEstimator(glm_family=...)`). As a result, NDE with a pre-instantiated second-stage model still fails or uses the wrong configuration.

Because this branch reuses the caller's estimator object directly, constructing `TwoStageRegressionEstimator` now permanently overwrites the passed estimator's `_target_estimand`. Reusing the same pre-instantiated estimator in another estimation (or in another stage) will silently run against the wrong estimand; this should clone the estimator instead of mutating caller-owned state.

The new `fit_params` plumbing is a behavioral change, but the added regression test doesn’t exercise the `method_params={"fit_params": ...}` path (including edge cases like `None`). Adding a small test that passes `method_params={"fit_params": {"effect_modifier_names": ...}}` (and/or `{"fit_params": None}`) would lock in the intended behavior and prevent regressions.

This test appears to rely on random data generation but doesn’t set a seed/random state, which can make the assertion flaky across environments. If `linear_dataset()` supports a `random_state`/`seed` argument, pass it here; otherwise, set the global RNG seed (e.g., NumPy) immediately before dataset creation to make the test deterministic.

`init_params` aliases the caller-provided `method_params` dict, so `pop("fit_params", ...)` mutates user input. Also, if a caller passes `{"fit_params": None}`, then `fit_params` becomes `None` and `**fit_params` will raise `TypeError`. Consider copying before mutation (e.g., `init_params = dict(method_params)`) and normalizing `fit_params` to `{}` when it is `None`.

The warning message and the re-raised `ValueError` message both encode similar “how to fix” guidance in separate hardcoded strings. To reduce duplication and keep future edits consistent, consider factoring the shared guidance text into a small constant or helper method (e.g., `_effect_modifiers_help_message()`), and reuse it in both the warning and exception path.

The detection for the EconML error is brittle: it only matches the exact substring `"X=None"`. If EconML changes the message (e.g., `"X is None"`, different casing, extra spaces, or a different phrasing), the improved guidance won’t trigger and users will still see the opaque error. Consider normalizing the message (e.g., remove spaces / lowercase) and matching a broader pattern (e.g., `"xnone"` or both `"x=none"` and `"x is none"`), or using a regex to reduce false negatives.
`from dowhy.causal_estimator import CausalEstimator` is unused here. Please remove the unused import to reduce noise in the test.
`group_sizes` is assigned but never used in this test. Please remove it to keep the test focused and avoid dead code.

The new tests cover scalar p-values and the bootstrap boundary tuples, but they don't cover the existing case where `p_value` is an `np.ndarray` (e.g., `LinearRegressionEstimator._test_significance` returns an array when there are multiple treatment variables). Since the updated formatter now uses a scalar float format (`{:.4g}`), adding a test for array-like `p_value` would prevent regressions for multi-treatment estimates.

For tuple p-values, the code assumes `lo==0` implies `p < hi` and otherwise treats it as `p > lo` and always marks it `Not significant`. If a tuple is ever something other than the bootstrap boundary cases `(0, hi)` or `(lo, 1)`, this can misclassify significance (with only a lower bound, you can only conclude "Not significant" when `lo >= alpha`). Suggest using both bounds to derive the conclusion (or falling back to a neutral label like "Inconclusive" when the bounds straddle alpha).

`signif_results_tostr` now formats non-tuple p-values with `"{:.4g}".format(pval)`, which raises `TypeError` when `pval` is a NumPy array (e.g., `LinearRegressionEstimator._test_significance` returns `np.ndarray` when there are multiple treatment variables). This will break `CausalEstimate.__str__` for multi-treatment estimates. Consider detecting array-like p-values (e.g., `np.ndarray`/list) and formatting them safely (or formatting each element) instead of using a scalar float format.

`observations` is assigned but never used in this test; consider renaming it to `_` to make the intent clear and avoid carrying unused locals.
```suggestion
    causal_model, _ = _create_and_fit_simple_probabilistic_causal_model()
```

`average_causal_effect` also has ValueError messages later in the function that mention `observed_samples`, but the parameter is called `observed_data`. Please align the error text with the actual parameter names (and with the updated `random_seed` docstring style).

In this function, the ValueError messages later in the body still refer to `observed_samples`, but the actual parameter name is `observed_data`. Please update those messages to avoid confusing users (and keep the wording consistent with `observed_data` vs `num_samples_to_draw`).

Fixed in commit 822d166. `_refute_once_with_estimator` now computes `effective_chosen_variables` (= `chosen_variables` + `['simulated']` when `unobserved_confounder_values` is not None) before calling `preprocess_data_by_treatment`, and uses that list for all subsequent `X_train_df`/`X_validation_df` indexing so the `'simulated'` column is no longer silently dropped.
@copilot fix this bug please

Updated in commit 8190e8a. The warning message no longer suggests `dropna()`. It now reads:

> "To address this, consider (1) understanding and correcting the source of missing values in the data generation process, or (2) explicitly modelling the missingness as part of the causal analysis."

Also applied the vectorized `data[relevant_cols].isna().any()` improvement suggested in the review.

@copilot dropping data can create a bias in the dataset.  a preferable mitigation would be to (1) understand where the NaN values are coming from and correcting the data generation process; or (2) model the missingness of data explicitly as part of the causal analysis.  Please update the message text appropriately

`warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")` mutates the global warnings filter for the remainder of the test run, which can inadvertently hide warnings (and related failures) in later tests. Prefer using `warnings.catch_warnings()`/`warnings.simplefilter(...)` in a context manager or a targeted pytest warning filter so the suppression is scoped to this test.
```suggestion
        with warnings.catch_warnings():
            warnings.simplefilter("ignore")
            data = dowhy.datasets.linear_dataset(
                beta=10,
                num_common_causes=1,
                num_instruments=0,
                num_treatments=1,
                num_samples=500,
                treatment_is_binary=True,
            )
            model = dowhy.CausalModel(
                data=data["df"],
                treatment=data["treatment_name"],
                outcome=data["outcome_name"],
                graph=data["gml_graph"],
            )
            estimand = model.identify_effect()
            estimate = model.estimate_effect(
                estimand,
                method_name="backdoor.linear_regression",
                evaluate_effect_strength=True,
            )
```

`estimate_effect_naive` hard-codes `control_value=0`/`treatment_value=1` and filters rows with `== 0/1`. If a caller runs `estimate_effect(..., control_value=..., treatment_value=...)` with non-default binary encodings, `evaluate_effect_strength` will compare against a naive estimate computed for the wrong values. Consider using the estimator’s current `self._control_value`/`self._treatment_value` (or accepting them as parameters) when building the naive estimate and when populating the returned `CausalEstimate`.
```suggestion
        df_withtreatment = data.loc[data[treatment_col] == self._treatment_value]
        df_notreatment = data.loc[data[treatment_col] == self._control_value]
        est = np.mean(df_withtreatment[outcome_col]) - np.mean(df_notreatment[outcome_col])
        return CausalEstimate(
            data,
            None,
            None,
            est,
            None,
            None,
            control_value=self._control_value,
            treatment_value=self._treatment_value,
        )
```

`estimate_effect_naive` now assumes a singleton treatment/outcome by indexing `[0]`, but it doesn't validate the length. With multiple treatments/outcomes, this will silently compute the naive estimate using only the first column (producing an incorrect `effect_strength`). Please add an explicit check that `len(treatment_variable)==1` and `len(outcome_variable)==1` (and raise a clear error if not supported).
```suggestion
        treatment_variable = self._target_estimand.treatment_variable
        outcome_variable = self._target_estimand.outcome_variable

        if len(treatment_variable) != 1:
            raise ValueError(
                "estimate_effect_naive only supports exactly one treatment variable; "
                f"got {len(treatment_variable)}: {treatment_variable}"
            )
        if len(outcome_variable) != 1:
            raise ValueError(
                "estimate_effect_naive only supports exactly one outcome variable; "
                f"got {len(outcome_variable)}: {outcome_variable}"
            )

        # Only supports a single binary treatment variable
        treatment_col = treatment_variable[0]
        outcome_col = outcome_variable[0]
```

The NDE regression test only checks the "Mediation" assumption string. Since the bug/fix also affects any assumption text that formats `outcome_nodes`, it would be better to assert the NDE case’s "Second-stage-unconfoundedness" contains the full outcome name (and does not contain the character-joined form), similar to the NIE test, to prevent regressions in that assumption too.
```suggestion
        assert "b,e,h,a,v,i,o,r" not in assumptions["Mediation"]
        assert "behavior" in assumptions["Second-stage-unconfoundedness"]
        assert "b,e,h,a,v,i,o,r" not in assumptions["Second-stage-unconfoundedness"]
```

This NaN detection scans columns one-by-one (`data[c].isna().any()` in a loop). You can compute this in a single vectorized pass over `data[relevant_cols]` to reduce repeated indexing overhead (especially for many treatments/outcomes) and simplify the code.
```suggestion
    nan_cols = data[relevant_cols].isna().any()
    nan_cols = nan_cols[nan_cols].index.tolist()
```

The warning suggests calling `data.dropna()` which drops rows with NaNs in *any* column, even though the check is intentionally limited to treatment/outcome columns. To keep the guidance accurate and avoid encouraging overly-destructive cleaning, suggest using a subset-based drop (e.g., dropping NaNs only in the affected treatment/outcome columns).
```suggestion
            "This may produce a NaN estimate. Consider calling data.dropna(subset=%s) before estimation.",
            nan_cols,
            relevant_cols,
```

The docstring claims that if `n_jobs=1` then 'no parallel computing code is used at all', but the implementation still calls `joblib.Parallel(n_jobs=1, ...)` in both paths. Either adjust the docstring to state that `n_jobs=1` runs sequentially via joblib's backend, or add an explicit non-joblib fast path for `n_jobs==1` to match the documented behavior (and reduce overhead).

Parallelizing simulations that rely on global RNG (e.g., pandas `.sample()` inside `_refute_once_with_estimator`) can accidentally reduce randomness or produce repeated splits across workers on some platforms/backends because worker processes may start from similar RNG states. To keep statistical properties consistent and improve reproducibility, consider explicitly seeding per-simulation randomness (e.g., derive a distinct seed from the simulation index and pass it through to every `.sample(random_state=...)` used in the simulation).

`preprocess_data_by_treatment()` mutates the `chosen_variables` list by appending `'simulated'` when `unobserved_confounder_values` is provided. Passing `list(chosen_variables)` prevents the mutation (good), but the subsequent indexing still uses the original `chosen_variables`, so `'simulated'` will be omitted from `X_train_df`/`X_validation_df` even though the column is added to the data. This changes the dummy outcome generation behavior whenever `unobserved_confounder_values` is used in the estimator-present path. Fix by ensuring the feature list used for `X_train_df`/`X_validation_df` includes `'simulated'` when applicable (e.g., compute an `effective_chosen_variables` once, or refactor `preprocess_data_by_treatment` to return the augmented list instead of mutating its input).

The `test_fraction` warning in the `not estimator_present` path is now only emitted when `unobserved_confounder_values is not None`, but `test_fraction` is still ignored in this path regardless. This is a behavior regression from the previous structure where the warning would trigger whenever `test_fraction` was set away from default. Move the `test_fraction != DEFAULT_TEST_FRACTION` warning outside the `unobserved_confounder_values` conditional so users are warned consistently.
```suggestion
        if test_fraction != DEFAULT_TEST_FRACTION:
            logger.warning("'test_fraction' is not applicable as there is no base treatment value.")

        # Handle unobserved confounder values before the parallel loop to avoid repeated mutations.

```

In Python 3, explicitly inheriting from `object` is redundant. You can simplify this to `class TestIdentifyMediation:` (no behavior change).
```suggestion
class TestIdentifyMediation:
```

This test documents that a warning is logged when no constraints can be run, but it doesn't assert on the warning itself. Consider using `caplog` to assert that the expected warning message is emitted, so the test actually locks in the warning-path behavior.
```suggestion
    def test_no_constraints_run_gives_warning_pass(self, caplog):
        """If no tests were run (both lists empty) the refuter logs a warning and passes."""
        refuter = self._refuter_with(true_n=0, false_n=0)
        with caplog.at_level("WARNING"):
            refuter.set_refutation_result(number_of_constraints_model=1)
        # When false_implications is empty but true_implications < model count,
        # the code falls through to the second elif (false_implications == 0) → passes.
        assert refuter._refutation_passed is True
        assert any(
            "no constraints" in message.lower() and "run" in message.lower()
            for message in caplog.messages
        )
```

This assertion is effectively tautological for supported configurations (`refutation_result` is always a boolean), and it would still pass even if the mixed-type routing fell into the NotImplemented/warning branch (which still sets a passing boolean). To actually validate the intended branch, assert that `refuter._results` contains the expected key and that the stored statistic is not `None`/"NotImplemented".
```suggestion
        constraint = ("X", "Y", ("Z",))
        result = refuter.refute_model(independence_constraints=[constraint])
        # Verify the mixed-type branch produced a concrete recorded result for this constraint.
        assert constraint in refuter._results
        statistic = refuter._results[constraint]
        assert statistic is not None
        assert statistic != "NotImplemented"
        assert result.refutation_result in {True, False}
```

`pytest` is imported but not used anywhere in this module. Consider removing it to avoid unused-import lint noise.

The discrete-path tests rely on `GraphRefuter.conditional_mutual_information`, which currently calls `dowhy.utils.cit.conditional_MI`. In the current implementation, `conditional_MI` indexes columns via `data[list(x)]`/`data[list(y)]` for string column names, which returns a DataFrame and makes the downstream entropy/zip logic behave incorrectly (often yielding a non-positive CMI and causing dependent cases to be classified as independent). These tests are likely to fail or provide false confidence unless the `conditional_MI` indexing is fixed (e.g., Series lookup by string key) or the tests are marked xfail/skipped until that fix is included.

`__setstate__` always recreates the logger using this module’s `__name__`, which will overwrite any estimator/subclass that configured a custom logger name/level before pickling/deepcopy. Consider persisting the original logger identity (e.g., store `self.logger.name` (and optionally `level`) into the serialized state before popping `logger`) and using that when recreating the logger in `__setstate__`.
```suggestion
        logger_obj = state.get("logger")
        if logger_obj is not None:
            state["_logger_name"] = logger_obj.name
            state["_logger_level"] = logger_obj.level
        state.pop("logger", None)
        return state

    def __setstate__(self, state):
        """Restore state and recreate the logger after unpickling."""
        logger_name = state.pop("_logger_name", __name__)
        logger_level = state.pop("_logger_level", None)
        self.__dict__.update(state)
        self.logger = logging.getLogger(logger_name)
        if logger_level is not None:
            self.logger.setLevel(logger_level)
```

The docstring asserts that NaN rows in the target are dropped, but the test only checks that `fit` doesn’t raise. To actually validate the documented behavior, add an assertion that demonstrates the fit result is equivalent to fitting on `data_with_nan.dropna(subset=[\"X1\"])` (e.g., compare downstream sampling output validity or compare learned model parameters/behavior where accessible).
```suggestion
    data = _simple_data(n=300, seed=42)
    # Introduce NaN in X1 (the non-root target)
    data_with_nan = data.copy()
    data_with_nan.loc[[10, 20, 30], "X1"] = np.nan
    data_without_nan = data_with_nan.dropna(subset=["X1"])

    scm_with_nan = _simple_scm()
    scm_without_nan = _simple_scm()

    # Fitting with NaN in the target should be equivalent to fitting on the
    # data after dropping rows where that target is NaN.
    fit(scm_with_nan, data_with_nan)
    fit(scm_without_nan, data_without_nan)

    # Align the root-node fit in both models so the comparison focuses on the
    # documented target-row-dropping behavior for X1.
    fit_causal_model_of_target(scm_with_nan, "X0", data_without_nan)
    fit_causal_model_of_target(scm_without_nan, "X0", data_without_nan)

    np.random.seed(123)

    np.random.seed(123)
    samples_without_nan = draw_samples(scm_without_nan, 50)

    pd.testing.assert_frame_equal(samples_with_nan, samples_without_nan)
```

```suggestion
        treatment = data[treatment_name]

```

Consider accumulating the ATT *sum* directly (or multiplying by `total_treated_matched`) and making the ATE weighting consistent with whatever units were actually matched; alternatively, raise/warn when some treated units have no eligible controls under exact matching.

This change alters the statistical decision rule by introducing a chi-squared p-value computation, but existing tests only assert overall pass/fail outcomes for random graphs. Using `df = max(1, ...)` masks degenerate cases where `x_card <= 1` or `y_card <= 1` (or `z_card == 0`), for which the chi-squared approximation isn’t meaningful. Consider explicitly handling constant variables/empty strata (e.g., treat as non-rejection with `p_value=1.0`, or return `NotImplemented`) rather than forcing `df=1`.
```suggestion
        df = (x_card - 1) * (y_card - 1) * z_card

        if x_card <= 1 or y_card <= 1 or df <= 0:
            # Degenerate contingency structure: the chi-squared approximation is not meaningful.
            # Treat this as a non-rejection instead of forcing df=1.
            p_value = 1.0
        else:

```

The degrees-of-freedom calculation uses cardinalities from the raw dataframe (`nunique()` / `drop_duplicates()`), but `conditional_MI` internally casts variables to `int` before computing CMI. For non-int columns (e.g., continuous or discretized-on-the-fly cases), this can make `g_stat` reflect the int-cast data while `df` reflects the original data, producing invalid p-values. Compute `x_card`, `y_card`, and `z_card` from the same transformed/discretized data that is used to compute CMI (or refactor to compute both CMI and df from a shared contingency table).

`conditional_MI` expects `x` and `y` to be list-like column name containers (it indexes with `data[list(x)]`). Passing `x`/`y` as strings will be treated as an iterable of characters (e.g., 'income' -> ['i','n',...]) and can cause incorrect column selection or KeyErrors. Wrap singleton variables as `[x]`/`[y]` (and similarly ensure `z` is a list of column names) before calling `conditional_MI`.

Note the 'regression_estimator' argument accepts a RegressionEstimator, whereas the 'propensity_score_model' argument accepts a propensity score model directly.

This is because it seemed more natural to let the user pass in a propensity score model directly, rather than a PropensityScoreEstimator, given most of the arguments, and all of the subclasses of the PropensityScoreEstimator class would be unneeded here (there would be no reason to pass in a PropensityScoreMatchingEstimator vs a PropensityScoreWeightingEstimator; the underlying propensity score model is working the same in all subclasses). Thus, it seemed more straightforward to just have the user pass in directly here the arguments that would be sufficient to handle the propensity score stage.

On the other hand, it seemed fair to use an argument of type RegressionEstimator for the regression stage, since a user may want to use a linear model, a glm, or some other future sub-class of the RegressionEstimator. In other words, the subclasses of RegressionEstimator may actually have their own constructors, that we wouldn't want to generalize here.

Yes, we use the train sample to fit the regression / weights, and then we "predict" the multiply-robust score on the evaluation sample and average that (as explained at the bottom of page 11 [here](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08839))

Note that we are using `sklearn` in `dowhy.gcm.ml`. We have a wrapper accordingly: https://github.com/py-why/dowhy/blob/main/dowhy/gcm/ml/regression.py#L28

Here, you can simply use any sklearn model: `prediction_model = lambda: SklearnRegressionModel(HistGradientBoostingRegressor())` (or in your case the `lightgbm` model). Generally, we assume the predictions to be 2D arrays within GCM. In that sense, your code should assume 2D outputs (i.e., it should assume non-flattened outputs). You can, of course, reshape it however you need it, but generally, the prediction model outputs 2D shapes to support multivariate outputs (although this is not really the case yet).

So, in many places, if we need flattened outputs, we are simply doing `my_model.predict(X).reshape(-1)`.

There is no cross-validation / model selection, but in the paper we recommend fitting the nuisance functions (regressions and weights) in a train sample, and then using a separate evaluation sample to compute the target parameter, either with simple sample splitting or with cross-fitting.

In this regard, I am wondering if the easier approach here is to simply assume that these models are already implemented with these `args` set. This is currently a lot of parameters to just configure the models. For instance:

```
regressor=LinearRegression,
regressor_args=(),
regressor_kwargs=None,
regressor_fit_kwargs=None
```

can be reduced to just
```
regressor: PredictionModel = create_linear_regressor
```
(with imports from gcm.ml.regression)

Now, if someone wants to have their custom parameters here, they can provide a custom implementation:

```

class MyLinearRegWithFancyParamters(SklearnRegressionModel):
   
   def fit(X, Y):
      mdl = LinearRegression()

      mdl.fit(X, Y, my_param1="..", my_param2=".....")
...

regressor = MyLinearRegWithFancyParamters
```

While this is maybe a bit more cumbersome than via the `args`, it would make the method definition cleaner (i.e., it removes 9 parameters). In practice, I would assume that only a small fraction of users would even use non-default parameters here.

Ah, ok I think I got the issue here. First I thought it was something like:
```
def method1():
    print("a")


def method2(**kwargs):
    method1(**kwargs)
```
Which works as long as no parameter is actually passed to `method2`. But here the issue is that the parameters for `validate_lmc` and `validate_parental_dsep` are/can be different, so the passed arguments in `kwargs` would need to be absorbed and ignored in the methods where they have no usage? This can be quite confusing then. If non-default parameters should be used in certain methods, then what about using `partial` to 'pre-configure' them, i.e.:

```
from functools import partial


def method1(my_optional_parameter=None):
    print("a")

    if my_optional_parameter is not None:
        print(my_optional_parameter)

partial(method1, my_optional_parameter="Test")()
```
`partial` here makes it a callable with the specific parameters pre-set (alternatively, one can also just use `lambda`). This is also the way how we do it with e.g. the independence tests when they need to be configured. I definitely see that it is more complicated for users, but probably better than introducing `kwargs` just to ignore unknown parameters.

So here it would be e.g.:
```
validate_graph(
    causal_graph=G_prime,
    data=data,
    causal_graph_reference=G,
    methods=(partial(validate_lmc, significance_level=0.1),
             partial(validate_parental_dsep, causal_graph_reference=some_graph)),
)
```

What do you think?

Say we want do use `validate_graph` on some graph `G_prime` that is a permutation of `G` (as done in `_permutation_based`). For this we call:
```
validate_graph(
    causal_graph=G_prime,
    data=data,
    causal_graph_reference=G,
    methods=(validate_lmc, validate_parental_dsep),
)
```
In `validate_graph` the `kwargs` are passed to the methods, i.e. `validate_lmc` and `validate_parental_dsep`. But if `validate_lmc` doesn't accept any `kwargs` even though they are not used internally, we would get: `TypeError: validate_lmc() got an unexpected keyword argument 'causal_graph_reference'`. Of course we could set an argument `causal_graph_reference=None` for `validate_lmc`(and likewise `validate_pd` and `validate_causal_minimality`) but I thought this would be very confusing. The same logic applies to why `validate_parental_dsep` requires `kwargs` (to absorb e.g. `data` when called via `validate_graph`).

But the `kwargs` are not used in this function at all (i.e., it is not passed to anything). The same applies to other functions as well. So, if the `kwargs` are not passed further, then we can just remove them from the parameter list. I only see `validate_graph` that uses the `kwargs` by passing it to another method.
I renamed this to `significance_level` as this is really what it is, the `significance_level` for the permutation-based test. I also added an explanation in the docstring of the method.
I renamed `evaluate_graph` to `falsify_graph` as this is more correct, easier to distinguish and in-line with the example notebook I wrote.

The `kwargs` are used here because we may want to pass additional arguments to some validation method. E.g. when wer run a permutation test (`_permutation_based`) we want to also pass the reference (unpermuted) graph to `validate_parental_dsep` via `'validate_graph`. However, this is not used by the other validation methods (e.g. `validate_lmc`). `kwargs` absorbs these arguments in such cases. Is there a better solution to this?

need_conditional_estimates: Boolean flag on whether treatment effect estimates conditional on the effect modifiers are needed. Otherwise, the average treatment effect is returned.

data: dataframe containing the data on which treatment effect is to be estimated.
treatment_value: value of the treatment variable for which the effect is to be estimated.
control_value: value of the treatment variable that denotes its absence (usually 0)
target_units: The units for which the treatment effect should be estimated. It can be a DataFrame that contains values of the effect_modifiers and effect will be estimated only for this new data. For context, the earlier version had this code inside the init method of the estimator. But now the init method does not get access to the data, only fit method gets access to the data (as in sklearn API). That's why this method needed to be created. But it may not be needed.

That's right, one need to check if the model ```has_coefficients``` actually, since this is what you need in the linear solution. I see the concern here. However, it would probably simplify the usage seeing that, if we have ```has_coefficients```, then the ```_linear``` version represents the closed-form solution that avoids the Shapley estimation. In that sense, there is no reason to use the ```_nonlinear``` version with a linear model.

I guess the question here is: Do we want to go a route where the methods automatically optimize the computation based on the model type (which requires ugly things like ```if isinstance(x, y)```) or should this be the responsibility of the user?

Under a single method, a user still has to select an appropriate wrapper (`SklearnLinearRegressionModel` or `SklearnRegressionModel`) for sklearn models, and pass its object. At this point, just calling a more explicit API is better, no? It is also more transparent. A bit more theoretical argument: With `isinstance` pattern, if we add new model types in the future, we have to keep on adding conditions and change this one method. 

The two arguments `foreground_mechanism` and `background_mechanism` does not make sense anymore when `analyze_inputs_only` is set to True.

The issue with creating abstract methods here is that we would essentially set the method signature (including parameters) so we would make all the effect and fit methods take the same parameters for all estimators (preventing new estimators to take different parameters or having a bunch of unneeded parameters in all estimators), even if python would allow for signatures to be different it is a bad practice and type checkers like mypy would complain about it. The solution (which is I think also the solution that scikit-learn uses) is to avoid defining the `fit` and `effect` in the base class, in fact in https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.base.BaseEstimator.html you can see that they don't have the fit and predict defined. I found this (https://scikit-learn.org/stable/developers/develop.html) to be pretty useful as help for this refactor, obviously it would be a giant effort (and perhaps not required) to make it work the same way as scikit-learn but it is a good guide.

This method needs to be wrapped inside/moved to the fit method. 
It was useful earlier to prevent re-fitting because we did not have an explicit fit method. 

We need to support three usecases in the fit method:
1. user provides their own scores in the propensity_score_column of dataframe. In that case, fit is a no-operation.
2. user provides model. In that case, use the user's model to fit. If the user has provided an already fitted model, then it is their responsibility to not call `fit` for the estimator. 
3. user does not provide model. In that case, use the default model to fit. 

Specifically, I suggest the following logic: If user provides the propensity score column and that column exists in the data, then do not fit anything. If user provides column name but column does not exist, then we check if user has provided propensity score model. If user has provided model, then we set `self.propensity_score_model` to that class, otherwise logisticregression is default. After that, we fit the model. 

Then in estimate_effect, we check again whether the propensity_score_column exists in the data (same check). If it does, we do nothing. If it does not, we call the self.propensity_score_model.predict_proba and fill the values of the propensity score column.

To summarize, 
1. fit method fits the propensity model.
2. estimate effect method uses the fitted model to fill the propensity score, if not filled already.

all of the above logic can be obtained by moving/restructuring the code inside `refresh_propensity_score`.
Also, finally, recalculate_propensity_score variable can be removed.

Technically speaking, the mechanisms used here are deterministic ones, which are just the functions in usual additive noise models or more concretely `PredictionModel`. But there are subtle differences due to which I would not use `PredictionModel` for this API. 

- `Clone` interface of `PredictionModel` is not necessary for this use case. 
- I deliberately avoided the fitting part to keep the API lean as users may fit the prediction model on one data set, but ask for attribution on a different data set. Our APIs should be doing less of usual ML tasks like hyperparameter tuning, cross-validation, data pre-processing, etc to fit a function, where ever we do that now, we should find a way to detach them in the future if possible.

My recommendation would be to inherit the `CanEvaluateInput` protocol by both `InvertibleFunction` and `PredictionModel`.  `evaluate` method of `InvertibleFunction` and `predict` method of `PredictionModel` have the same signature, yet they just differ in semantics. Note that `predict` method is only used internally so renaming it to `evaluate` would not be a big issue.

@andresmor-ms @amit-sharma Hey guys, I was wondering how you think about the following proposal (which is what I believe is what we have also discussed in the past at some point). It might be a bit naiv, because I don't understand all the details and subtleties of the existing implementation. But just throwing it out there:

```python
estimator = PropensityScoreMatchingEstimator(
    identified_estimand=identified_estimand,
    ... # I think most parameters are only needed during effect estimation, so they should not be necessary here
    effect_modifiers=graph.get_effect_modifiers(treatment_name, outcome_name),
)

estimator.fit(data=data["df"])

estimate = estimate_effect(
    treatment=treatment_name,
    outcome=outcome_name,
    identified_estimand=identified_estimand, # do we even need this here again, given that this is already in the esitmator?
    identifier_name="backdoor",
    method=estimator,
    treatment_value=...,
    control_value=...
)
```
What I like about this proposal is that it separates the fitting from effect estimation. Still, to make this less verbose:
```python
estimate = estimate_effect(
    treatment=treatment_name,
    outcome=outcome_name,
    identified_estimand=identified_estimand,
    identifier_name="backdoor",
    method=PropensityScoreMatchingEstimator(
        identified_estimand=identified_estimand,
        ... 
        effect_modifiers=graph.get_effect_modifiers(treatment_name, outcome_name),
    ).fit(data=data["df"]),
    treatment_value=...,
    control_value=...
)
```
This assumes that `fit(...)` returns `self`. This would preserve the property of the current DoWhy API where effect estimation is one step.

Finally, since `estimate_effect` ultimately calls the estimators `estimate_effect`, do we even need the free function `estimate_effect`? What exactly does it provide?

If we don't need such function, we could even simplify to:
```python
estimate = PropensityScoreMatchingEstimator(
        identified_estimand=identified_estimand,
        ... 
        effect_modifiers=graph.get_effect_modifiers(treatment_name, outcome_name),
    ) \
    .fit(data=data["df"]) \
    .estimate_effect(treatment=treatment_name,
                     outcome=outcome_name,
                     identified_estimand=identified_estimand,
                     identifier_name="backdoor",
                     treatment_value=...,
                     control_value=...
```

I think the logic of checking "has_directed_path" here is that it is an early sanity check that the caller is asking a sensible question.  I.e., if a variable X has no way of influencing Y according to the causal graph, then it doesn't make sense to do a fancy calculation to give you the same response.  It's perhaps more likely that the caller is making a mistake somewhere and we want to call that out.

So, if we have a higher cardinality, I can see two options: 
(1) a minimum requirement would be that at least one of the treatments has a direct path to at least one of the outcomes.
(2) more aggressively:  every one of the treatments has at least one direct path to one of the outcomes.  And, every one of the outcomes has a direct path from at least one of the treatments.

I'd probably go for #2

This is a good point. For all the partial-r2 based methods (linear and non-parametric), it has the same interpretation. 
But the interpretation is different between direct_simulation and partial-r2 based simulations. 

So to avoid confusion, I have created two additional parameters. The first two (effect_strength) are used for direct_simulation, while the next two (partial_r2_confounder) are used for the partial-r2 based methods.

This is still a hacky solution. The ideal solution will be achieved in the new API where we can wrap the method specific parameters within the call to the method class. (and create separate method names and remove the simulation method parameter).

```
refute_estimate(..., method=AddSimulatedConfounder(effect_strength_on_treatment=a, effect_strength_on_outcome=b))
# if we want to use partial R2 methods
refute_estimate(..., method=NonParametricPartialR2Sensitivity(partial_r2_confounder_treatment=a, partial_r2_confounder_outcome=b))
```

This else condition is the scenario where we don't have input features, i.e. empty conditioning set. Then we have: ```E[Y | {}] = E[Y]```. Here, ```Y``` is based on ```Y_training```. Therefore, the prediction model we are looking at here is ```f({}) = E[Y | {}] = E[Y]``` and, thus, the sum of squared error in the test data is ```sum_i (y_i - f({}))**2 = sum_i (y_i - E[Y])**2```

Hi Amit! Here go some extra clarifications.

Consider the example in the notebook, and suppose the treatment, whether someone does warm up exercises, is binary, and so is the outcome, whether they have injuries after playing sports. 

Suppose a researcher is interested in the causal effect that prescribing players to do warm-up exercises has on injury. If the hypothetical intervention is to make all players (regardless of their individual characteristics) do warm up exercises, then the causal effect can be quantified via the ATE, the difference in the expected outcomes in two “parallel” worlds, one in which everyone does warm up exercises (regardless of the player’s individual characteristics), the other in which no one does warm up exercises. In this case the conditional nodes are the empty set (there are none), because the intervention is carried out with no regard to individual characteristics.

Now, perhaps a different, individualised, intervention is of interest. That is, the researcher might be interested in estimating the injury probability in a counterfactual world, in which players are prescribed to perform warm up exercises according to some of their characteristics. One such policy could for example be, apply treatment (warm up =1) only when the patient has previous injury = 1 and team motivation <= 6. Another one could be, if the person has tissue disorder then flip a fair coin and if it lands heads have them to warm up exercises, if it lands tails then have them don’t do warm up exercises. Notice that this latter treatment rule is randomised. For the first case, the conditional nodes are previous injury and team motivation, for the second case the only conditional node is tissue disorder.

Thus, conditional nodes can be any set of non-descendants of treatment (including the empty set); they need not be parents of the treatment.  Changing the conditional nodes changes what the target estimand is.

Suppose now we built a graph, computed an adjustment set (possibly for an individualised treatment rule), and have collected data on the treatment, the conditional nodes and the outcome. Then we can estimate the expectation of the counterfactual outcome under our treatment rule of interest using any of the usual estimation strategies (IPW, outcome regression, AIPW, etc). To make matters more concrete, let Y be the outcome, A the treatment,  L=tissue disorder, and Z be the adjustment set we got from running any of the algorithms on the graph. Let pi(A |L) be the randomised treatment rule based on tissue disorder we described before, that is pi(A=1 |L)= 0.5 if L=tissue_disorder=1 and pi(A=1 |L)= 0 if  L=tissue_disorder=0. Let f^hat(A| Z) the estimated propensity score. Then an estimate of the expected outcome in the counterfactual world in which people are allocated to treatment according to the pi(A |L) treatment rule is given by 
average ( Y * pi(A|L) /  f^hat(A| Z) ). This is the IPW estimator; the other ones are defined analogously. Notice that if pi(A|L) did not depend on L, say pi(A|L)=1 when A=1, regardless of the value of L, then this is just the usual formula for the IPW.

```py
DoWhy | An end-to-end library for causal inference
===================================================

  Introducing DoWhy and the 4 steps of causal inference | `Microsoft Research Blog <https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/blog/dowhy-a-library-for-causal-inference/>`_ | `Video Tutorial <https://note.microsoft.com/MSR-Webinar-DoWhy-Library-Registration-On-Demand.html>`_ | `Arxiv Paper <https://arxiv.org/abs/2011.04216>`_ | `Slides <https://www2.slideshare.net/AmitSharma315/dowhy-an-endtoend-library-for-causal-inference>`_

  Read the `docs <https://py-why.github.io/dowhy/>`_ | Try it online! |Binder|_ 

.. |Binder| image:: https://mybinder.org/badge_logo.svg
.. _Binder: https://mybinder.org/v2/gh/microsoft/dowhy/master?filepath=docs%2Fsource%2F

**Case Studies using DoWhy**: `Hotel booking cancellations <https://towardsdatascience.com/beyond-predictive-models-the-causal-story-behind-hotel-booking-cancellations-d29e8558cbaf>`_ | `Effect of customer loyalty programs <https://github.com/microsoft/dowhy/blob/master/docs/source/example_notebooks/dowhy_example_effect_of_memberrewards_program.ipynb>`_ | `Optimizing article headlines <https://medium.com/@akelleh/introducing-the-do-sampler-for-causal-inference-a3296ea9e78d>`_ | `Effect of home visits on infant health (IHDP) <https://towardsdatascience.com/implementing-causal-inference-a-key-step-towards-agi-de2cde8ea599>`_ | `Causes of customer churn/attrition <https://medium.com/geekculture/a-quickstart-for-causal-analysis-decision-making-with-dowhy-2ce2d4d1efa9>`_

.. image:: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/microsoft/dowhy/master/docs/images/dowhy-schematic.png

As computing systems are more frequently and more actively intervening in societally critical domains such as healthcare, education, and governance, it is critical to correctly predict and understand the causal effects of these interventions. Without an A/B test, conventional machine learning methods, built on pattern recognition and correlational analyses, are insufficient for decision-making. 

Much like machine learning libraries have done for prediction, **"DoWhy" is a Python library that aims to spark causal thinking and analysis**. DoWhy provides a principled four-step interface for causal inference that focuses on explicitly modeling causal assumptions and validating them as much as possible. The key feature of DoWhy is its state-of-the-art refutation API that can automatically test causal assumptions for any estimation method, thus making inference more robust and accessible to non-experts. DoWhy supports estimation of the average causal effect for backdoor, frontdoor, instrumental variable and other identification methods, and estimation of the conditional effect (CATE) through an integration with the EconML library.  

For a quick introduction to causal inference, check out `amit-sharma/causal-inference-tutorial <https://github.com/amit-sharma/causal-inference-tutorial/>`_. We also gave a more comprehensive tutorial at the ACM Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (`KDD 2018 <http://www.kdd.org/kdd2018/>`_) conference: `causalinference.gitlab.io/kdd-tutorial <http://causalinference.gitlab.io/kdd-tutorial/>`_. For an introduction to the four steps of causal inference and its implications for machine learning, you can access this video tutorial from Microsoft Research: `DoWhy Webinar <https://note.microsoft.com/MSR-Webinar-DoWhy-Library-Registration-On-Demand.html>`_.

Documentation for DoWhy is available at `py-why.github.io/dowhy <https://py-why.github.io/dowhy/>`_.
```
